message.content:
You are an adversarial security reviewer. Your job is to REFUTE the following candidate vulnerability. Default to refuted=true unless the evidence is solid. Read the actual code at the cited path before deciding.

TARGET: chia-blockchain daemon WebSocket server  (repo root: C:/bugBounty/targets/chia-blockchain daemon WebSocket server)
THREAT MODEL: The daemon exposes a local WebSocket control server (server.py) plus keychain_server.py/keychain_proxy.py. Threat: a process or web page able to reach the daemon websocket, or weak auth, leading to command/argument injection when launching plotters/services (subprocess), arbitrary command exec, path traversal in file ops, or keychain access without authorization. Check websocket origin/auth validation and how command names/args from messages reach subprocess.

CANDIDATE FINDING:
  title: Authenticated daemon client can extract all private keys (mnemonics/entropy) over the WebSocket keychain RPC
  class: keychain access / sensitive data exposure (CWE-522)
  severity claimed: Low
  file: chia/daemon/keychain_server.py:317
  source: WebSocket message command="get_all_private_keys" handled in WebSocketServer.handle_message -> keychain_commands
  sink: Keychain.get_all_private_keys() returning sk + entropy, serialized as hex in the RPC response (get_all_private_keys, lines 317-326)
  data_flow: server.py:incoming_connection -> handle_message (command in keychain_commands) -> KeychainServer.handle_command("get_all_private_keys") -> get_all_private_keys() -> get_keychain_for_request(request).get_all_private_keys() -> response contains pk + entropy.hex(). The only gates are (a) mutual TLS (ssl_context_for_server sets verify_mode=CERT_REQUIRED with the node's private CA in chia/server/server.py:71) and (b) keyring unlocked. Any client presenting a cert signed by the local private CA therefore obtains full master secret material.
  excerpt: private_keys = self.get_keychain_for_request(request).get_all_private_keys()
for sk, entropy in private_keys:
    all_keys.append({"pk": bytes(sk.get_g1()).hex(), "entropy": entropy.hex()})

LENS = CONCRETE EXPLOIT. Construct the precise input/request/payload that triggers the issue and state the resulting impact. If you cannot produce a plausible working exploit from the actual code (or it requires unrealistic preconditions), set refuted=true.

Set is_real=true ONLY if, after reading the code, the finding survives your lens. Provide reason and, if real, an exploit_sketch and adjusted_severity. Return ONLY the structured object.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
The TLS gate is real: `verify_mode = CERT_REQUIRED` with the node's private CA. So a client must present a cert signed by the local Chia private CA to connect. This is the intended trust boundary for the daemon control channel.

The candidate's own data flow confirms: the only gates are (a) mutual TLS with the private CA and (b) keyring unlocked. The "vulnerability" is that an *authenticated* client (one already trusted by the local CA) can read keys.

This is by-design behavior. The keychain RPC exists precisely so the local wallet/CLI (which all share the same private CA) can manage keys including reading mnemonics for signing/backup. A client that holds a cert signed by the private CA is already a fully trusted local Chia component — the private CA key sits on the same machine. There is no privilege escalation: anyone with the private CA cert/key already has filesystem access to the keyring itself.

Let me confirm the CA files are local and that there's no separate per-client authorization that this bypasses.

content.text:
Confirmed. There's no per-message or per-client authorization beyond mutual TLS. Any client with a cert signed by the local private CA can call `get_all_private_keys`. But that is exactly the trust model: the CA private key and the keyring live on the same host, accessible to the daemon owner.

This is intended/by-design functionality (the wallet GUI and CLI legitimately fetch private keys this way for signing and backups). It is gated by mutual TLS against the node's private CA. An attacker holding a CA-signed cert already has local trusted access; possessing it implies local filesystem access to the keyring/CA material anyway — no privilege boundary is crossed. The "Low" severity even acknowledges this is not a real boundary violation.

The exploit requires an unrealistic precondition for a *vulnerability*: obtaining a client cert signed by the private CA, which is equivalent to already owning the keys. No working exploit exists for an actual attacker who isn't already a trusted local component.

input.reason: Code matches the excerpt: keychain_server.py:317-326 returns pk + entropy.hex() for all keys, reachable via server.py:436-437 (command in keychain_commands -> KeychainServer.handle_command). However, this is by-design functionality gated by a real trust boundary, not a vulnerability. server.py:199 builds the listener SSL context via ssl_context_for_server, which at chia/server/server.py:71 sets verify_mode = ssl.CERT_REQUIRED against the node's private CA. Thus only a client presenting a certificate signed by the local Chia private CA can even connect. That is precisely the intended trust model: the wallet GUI/CLI fetch private keys this way for signing and backups. An attacker capable of presenting a CA-signed client cert already possesses the private CA key, which lives on the same host as the keyring itself, so no privilege/trust boundary is crossed - obtaining the prerequisite cert is equivalent to already having local filesystem access to the secret material. The candidate's own data_flow concedes the only gates are mutual TLS and an unlocked keyring; there is no separate per-client authorization being bypassed. No working exploit exists for any party who is not already a fully trusted local component. The claimed 'Low' severity itself reflects that this is not a genuine boundary violation.
input.exploit_sketch: No realistic exploit. Triggering get_all_private_keys requires a TLS client cert signed by the node's private CA (chia/server/server.py:71 verify_mode=CERT_REQUIRED). Acquiring that cert/key requires local access to the same files/keyring the keys live in, so there is no privilege escalation or remote/unauthorized path.

content.text: Refuted. The finding describes intended functionality gated by mutual TLS against the node's private CA — not a vulnerability.
